title: Trees
description: Master C# binary tree implementation: TreeNode class, DFS traversals, BFS with Queue, and depth calculations for coding interviews.

sections.title: TreeNode Class Definition
sections.explanation: Define a binary tree node with `val`, `left`, and `right`. In C# interviews, you typically define this as a simple class at the top. Nodes are created with `new TreeNode(val)` and connected by setting `.Left` and `.Right`. Null represents an empty child. This is the foundation for all tree problems.

exercise.prompt:
Build this tree:       1
                     / \
                    2   3
                   /   / \
                  4   5   6
Print the root value, its left child, its right child's right child, and the total number of nodes (count manually or write a recursive count function).

validation.message: Build tree with new TreeNode, implement recursive CountNodes.

exercise.hint: CountNodes: if (node == null) return 0; return 1 + CountNodes(node.Left) + CountNodes(node.Right);

sections.title: DFS Traversals: Inorder, Preorder, Postorder
sections.explanation: DFS traverses trees depth-first using recursion. **Inorder** (Left, Root, Right): gives sorted order for BST. **Preorder** (Root, Left, Right): useful for serialization. **Postorder** (Left, Right, Root): useful for deletion and bottom-up computation. Each traversal visits every node exactly once in O(n) time.

exercise.prompt:
Build this BST:     5
                  / \
                 3   7
                / \   \
               1   4   9
Implement all three DFS traversals. Print inorder, preorder, and postorder results as comma-separated values on separate lines.

validation.message: Implement all three traversals with correct visit order.

exercise.hint: Preorder: add, left, right. Postorder: left, right, add. Inorder: left, add, right.

sections.title: BFS Level-Order Traversal
sections.explanation: BFS uses a Queue to traverse trees level by level. Pattern: enqueue root, while queue not empty: dequeue node, process it, enqueue left and right children. For level-by-level grouping, process `queue.Count` nodes per iteration. This is essential for problems like: level-order traversal, right-side view, zigzag traversal.

exercise.prompt:
Build the tree:     1
                  / \
                 2   3
                / \   \
               4   5   6
Implement level-order traversal that returns List<List<int>>. Print each level on its own line. Also print the number of levels.

validation.message: Level 0: 1, Level 1: 2,3, Level 2: 4,5,6, then 3 levels total.
Use Queue<TreeNode> for BFS with Enqueue and Dequeue.

exercise.hint: while queue.Count > 0: int size = queue.Count; loop size times: dequeue, add val, enqueue children.

sections.title: Tree Depth and Height
sections.explanation: Maximum depth (height) of a binary tree is the longest root-to-leaf path. Recursive formula: `MaxDepth(node) = 1 + Max(MaxDepth(left), MaxDepth(right))`. Base case: null returns 0. Can also compute iteratively using BFS (count levels). Minimum depth is similar but only counts paths to leaf nodes (both children null).

exercise.prompt: Build two trees. Tree A:  1→(2→(4,5), 3) (depth 3, balanced). Tree B: 1→(2→(3→(4,null),null),null) (depth 4, not balanced). For each tree, print the max depth and whether it is balanced.

validation.message: Tree A: depth 3, balanced. Tree B: depth 4, not balanced.
Use MaxDepth for depth calculation and check balance with height difference.

exercise.hint: IsBalanced: if null return true. Check |depth(left) - depth(right)| <= 1 AND both subtrees balanced.
